title: Bautista-Spille v. Nicorp Management and Development Corporation
holding: A general or administrative authority (or mere authority to negotiate/mortgage) does not include the power to sell real property; a sale of land by an agent without an express SPA to sell is void.

links.relevance: Authority to negotiate/mortgage is not authority to sell; the de la Fuente conveyance under a negotiate-only SPA is void.
links.note: Maps to PE-170453 negotiate-only SPA; parallels Cosmic Lumber/Alcantara.

links.relevance: Void-deed root: agent lacked express power to sell.
links.note: Core validity attack on T-079-2021002126 chain.

title: Tumibay v. Lopez
holding: Where the agent exceeds the limits of the SPA and the buyer is aware, the sale is void under Art. 1898 and reconveyance may be ordered.

links.relevance: Agent acting beyond SPA + buyer's awareness renders the sale void and supports reconveyance.
links.note: Supports reconveyance where transferees knew of the authority defect.

links.relevance: Void sale + reconveyance remedy.

title: Mactan-Cebu International Airport Authority v. Unchuan
holding: A sale by an agent without the required written SPA is void; an action to declare the inexistence of a void contract does not prescribe.

links.relevance: Action to declare the void de la Fuente conveyances inexistent does not prescribe.
Defeats prescription/laches against recovery.

title: Tolentino v. Latagan
holding: A forged deed of sale is null and void and conveys no title; protection arises only if a subsequent innocent purchaser for value acquires after registration.

links.relevance: A forged/void deed conveys no title; the void root taints the derivative titles.
links.note: Void-root doctrine for the T-52540 -> 079-2021002126 chain.

links.relevance: Void deed conveys nothing.

title: Peralta v. Abalon
holding: A forged or fraudulent deed is void and conveys no title, but an innocent purchaser for value acquiring from the registered owner after transfer may be protected, subject to clean-title/no-notice conditions.

links.relevance: Void deed conveys no title; IPV protection is the narrow exception the defendants must prove.
links.note: Frames the IPV burden onto the Balane side.

title: Sarili v. Lagrosa
holding: A buyer purchasing from one who is not the registered owner must verify not just the title but the seller's authority; failure to investigate authority (esp. on a forged/defective document) defeats innocent-purchaser status.

links.relevance: Transferees dealing through de la Fuente had a duty to verify his authority; failure defeats IPV.
links.note: Defeats the IPV defense across the transferee cluster.

links.relevance: Balane cannot claim IPV without proving investigation of the seller's authority.
links.note: Direct rebuttal to the anticipated clean-title defense.

title: Bucton v. Go
holding: Even a notarized SPA (presumed regular) does not excuse a buyer dealing through an agent from higher prudence; lack of verification bars innocent-purchaser status.

links.relevance: Notarization of the de la Fuente SPA does not cure the buyers' duty to verify authority.
Notarized SPA does not confer IPV status.

title: Manalese v. Ferreras
holding: Reliance on a clean title is not automatic; suspicious circumstances or irregularities impose a duty of further inquiry, and failure defeats innocent-purchaser protection.

links.relevance: Recent (2024) authority: suspicious circumstances defeat IPV reliance on a clean title.
links.note: Freshest controlling statement against the IPV defense.

title: Valenzuela v. Roman Catholic Archbishop of Manila
holding: In accion reivindicatoria the plaintiff must prove the identity of the land and the strength of his own title, not the weakness of the defendant's.

links.relevance: Elements Patricia must prove: identity of the parcel + strength of the T-4497-derived title.
Title-strength element ties to the T-4497 chain.

title: VSD Realty & Development Corporation v. Uniwide Sales
holding: The plaintiff in a reivindicatory action must definitely identify the land claimed by competent evidence (technical description/survey).

links.relevance: Requires competent identification of the parcel by technical description/survey.

title: Sampaco v. Lantud
holding: A Torrens title is conclusive evidence of ownership and indefeasible; overcoming it requires clear and convincing evidence of a recognized ground (e.g., fraud) in the proper action.

links.relevance: Frames both sides' title conclusiveness; our attack must be by direct action on the void root.
links.note: Guides posture: direct nullity action, not collateral attack.

title: Hutchison v. Buscas
holding: To recover in accion reivindicatoria the plaintiff must prove the identity of the land and rely on the strength of his own title.

links.relevance: Identity + own-title strength are indispensable.

title: Descallar v. Heirs of Feria Guevara
holding: Accion reivindicatoria requires showing the defendant is in possession without right and is withholding possession from the owner.

links.relevance: Establishes the withholding-of-possession element against the current occupants.

title: Cullado v. Gutierrez
holding: In a possession suit the court may resolve ownership only provisionally as needed for possession; it cannot make a final adjudication amounting to a collateral attack on a Torrens title.

links.relevance: Bounds what the MTC can decide on ownership in the summary case; shapes forum strategy.
links.note: Supports the wedge/test-and-reserve posture (final nullity belongs in RTC).
